title: Floquet analysis of coherence in periodically driven diamond NV ensemble systems
authors: Cuong M. Nguyen, Uijin Ko, Seong-Joo Lee, Hyeonsu Kim, Hosung Seo, Sangwon Oh
abstract: High-density nitrogen-vacancy (NV) ensembles are promising platforms for solid-state quantum sensing, but their performance is limited by dipolar interactions and inhomogeneous dephasing. Periodic decoupling sequences such as Waugh-Huber-Haeberlen (WAHUHA) can extend the observed stroboscopic decay time. However, it remains unclear that a longer effective dephasing time yield improved magnetic-field sensitivity. Here, we show that WAHUHA control increases the effective inhomogeneous dephasing time of a dense NV ensemble from $T_2^\ast$ of 0.9 $μ$s to $T_{2,eff}^\ast$ of 31 $μ$s, while producing little improvement in dc magnetic-field sensitivity. Using detuning-resolved stroboscopic spectroscopy and finite-pulse Floquet analysis, we show that the long-lived signal arises from phase wrapping and quasi-energy branch folding of the one-cycle unitary. These effects reshape the stroboscopic spectrum and suppress the detuning-to-phase transduction slope, $dΦ/dΔ$, which governs the dc magnetic-field response. Our results demonstrate that, under periodic driving, an extended effective dephasing time does not necessarily translate into enhanced dc sensitivity and establish finite-pulse Floquet analysis as a practical framework for evaluating coherence in spin ensembles.
summary: The research investigates how periodic driving sequences affect quantum coherence in NV ensembles used for magnetometry. While these sequences successfully extend the measurable dephasing time ($	ext{T}_2^*$), the authors demonstrate that this improvement does not automatically boost DC magnetic-field sensitivity. They establish that finite-pulse Floquet analysis is necessary to accurately predict sensing limits by focusing on the phase transduction slope rather than just the coherence envelope.
main_problem: To determine if extending the effective dephasing time ($	ext{T}_2^*$) via periodic driving in NV ensembles necessarily leads to improved DC magnetic-field sensitivity.
main_result: Extended coherence times are not guaranteed to enhance DC sensitivity because the signal is governed by the detuning-to-phase transduction slope ($d\Phi/d\Delta$), which can be suppressed by phase wrapping effects during periodic driving.
method: The study combines detuning-resolved stroboscopic spectroscopy with finite-pulse Floquet analysis to model coherence decay in periodically driven spin ensembles.
model_or_system: The system is a high-density Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) ensemble in diamond, modeled as an effective two-level or spin-1/2 system subjected to periodic control pulses like WAHUHA sequences. The dynamics are analyzed using the one-cycle unitary propagator derived from Floquet theory.
key_observables: Effective inhomogeneous dephasing time ($	ext{T}_2^*$), DC magnetic-field sensitivity, and the detuning-to-phase transduction slope ($d\Phi/d\Delta$).
important_parameters: The WAHUHA sequence period ($	au$) and the detuning ($\Delta$) are key parameters controlling the Floquet phase landscape.
main_assumptions: The analysis moves beyond ideal Average Hamiltonian Theory (AHT) to incorporate finite pulse widths and variable interpulse delays, which modify the one-cycle evolution.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate stroboscopic FFT spectra showing linear branches at short delays and characteristic period-doubled ($2T$) responses due to phase wrapping at longer delays. Signal slope maps compare Ramsey vs. WAHUHA protocols across varying delay times.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical system (NV ensembles) and the theoretical framework (Floquet analysis). It then analyzes how periodic driving extends coherence ($	ext{T}_2^*$) but critically demonstrates that this extension does not translate to better sensing performance because of spectral reshaping effects governed by $d\Phi/d\Delta$.
why_interesting: This work provides a crucial theoretical refinement for quantum sensing, showing that simply maximizing coherence time is insufficient; the specific mechanism coupling detuning to phase accumulation (the transduction slope) must be optimized.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
